metadata.title: Experiment Harness Improvement: Persistent Monitoring Infrastructure
metadata.approach: TDD — RED tests first, then GREEN implementation

failure-history.failure: 8 identical FAILED_SETUP jobs undetected for 2h 20m. No batch-level kill switch existed. A "3+ identical failures in 5 min → cancel all" rule would have saved $3.90.

Job #154 ran 12+ hours for a 5-minute training task. ralph_monitor.py tracks status transitions but NEVER compares elapsed time vs expected duration. A simple "duration > 3x expected → cancel" rule would have caught it at minute 15, saving $23+. Metalearning: .claude/metalearning/2026-03-27-no-job-duration-monitoring-12h-debug-run.md

3 Phase 1 jobs PENDING for 10+ hours. Claude Code session polled for 50 minutes then effectively ended. Zero escalation, zero fallback, zero external monitoring. User discovered it next morning. Metalearning: .claude/metalearning/2026-03-28-10hr-pending-no-monitoring-intervention.md

root-causes.rc: Monitoring is a Claude Code skill (markdown instructions), not a daemon. When the session ends, ALL monitoring stops. This is architectural.
ralph_monitor.py tracks status transitions but never compares elapsed duration against expected duration. No warn/cancel thresholds.
ralph_monitor.py watches ONE job. Factorial experiments launch 20-34 jobs. No script aggregates batch-level health or detects cascading failures.
No desktop notifications, no email, no Slack. The only "alerting" is printing to the Claude Code conversation, which requires an active session.
No runtime cost guard. A stuck job accumulates cost indefinitely. The harness estimates cost before launch but has no runtime enforcement.

constraints.constraint: Monitor MUST be a standalone script — NOT a Claude skill, NOT session-dependent
Must work without Claude Code running (the whole point of this plan)
Must work on Linux — notify-send for desktop notifications
Must parse `sky jobs queue` output reliably — Python str.split(), NOT regex (Rule 16)
Must handle SkyPilot API server being down gracefully
All tests in tests/v2/unit/ following project conventions
Monitor MUST be running BEFORE any job launch — new pre-launch gate
No GitHub Actions CI — all validation runs locally (Rule 21)
TDD MANDATORY — write failing tests FIRST (Rule 2)

context.user-authorization: User requested this plan on 2026-03-28 after reviewing the improvement report. All 4 phases are user-authorized for planning. Implementation requires /self-learning-iterative-coder execution.

expert.perspective:
The monitoring architecture should follow the three pillars: metrics (duration, cost time-series), logs (SkyPilot job logs), traces (cross-flow provenance). The immediate need is metrics — specifically, job duration vs. expected duration. This is a single time-series comparison, not a complex observability stack.
Alerting architecture: The monitor writes to JSONL (structured log), checks thresholds (metrics), and sends desktop notifications (escalation). This is a minimal but complete monitoring stack.
Escalation policy: - INFO: Status change logged to JSONL (no notification) - WARN: Duration > 3x expected → desktop notification + terminal bell - CRITICAL: Duration > max_duration → auto-cancel + desktop notification - EMERGENCY: Budget exceeded → cancel ALL jobs + desktop notification
The kill-switch (3+ identical failures) is a critical circuit breaker. Without it, cascading failures waste hours. Implement as a sliding window: track last N failures per error category, trigger if count >= 3 within 5 minutes.
IMPORTANT: The monitor script must be idempotent. If cron fires twice, the second invocation should detect the first is running (via PID file) and exit cleanly.

Implementation of monitor_factorial.py should follow clean separation:
1. Data layer: JobManifest dataclass (JSON serialization), JobRecord dataclass 2. Polling layer: SkyPilotPoller class wrapping subprocess calls to sky CLI 3. Detection layer: AnomalyDetector class with configurable thresholds 4. Alerting layer: AlertDispatcher class (notify-send + terminal + JSONL) 5. Orchestration: main loop tying them together
Testing strategy: Mock the SkyPilotPoller (it calls subprocess). Everything else is pure Python logic that can be tested without SkyPilot installed. Use pytest fixtures with realistic sky jobs queue output captured from actual runs.
Key design decisions: - Use dataclasses for all data structures (not dicts) - Use pathlib.Path for all file operations (Rule 6) - Use datetime.now(timezone.utc) for all timestamps (Rule 7) - from __future__ import annotations at the top (Rule 8) - encoding='utf-8' for all file operations (Rule 5) - No regex for parsing sky jobs queue (Rule 16) — use str.split() - No hardcoded thresholds — all from manifest/config (Rule 29)
The manifest generator (create_job_manifest.py) should read the factorial config YAML and the sky jobs queue output to produce the manifest JSON. This means it can be run AFTER launch (when job IDs are known) or BEFORE (with placeholders that get filled in by run_factorial.sh).
Test count estimate: 25-35 unit tests across 3 test files.

SkyPilot v1.0 `sky jobs queue` output format (from actual observation):
``` Managed jobs ID TASK NAME REQUESTED SUBMITTED TOT.DURATION JOB.DURATION #RECOVERIES STATUS POOL 161 - sam3_vanilla-zeroshot-minivess-f0 1x L4:1 2026-03-28 06:01 1h 22m - 3 PENDING sky-managed... 160 - sam3_hybrid-cbdice_cldice-calibfalse-f0 1x L4:1 2026-03-28 06:00 1h 23m - 1 PENDING sky-managed... 159 - dynunet-dice_ce-calibfalse-f0 1x L4:1 2026-03-28 05:59 1h 24m - 2 PENDING sky-managed... ```
Column layout (space-delimited, columns vary in width): - Column 0: ID (integer) - Column 1: TASK ("-" for managed jobs) - Column 2: NAME (the condition name from run_factorial.sh) - Column 3: REQUESTED (e.g., "1x L4:1" — may have spaces!) - Column 4+: SUBMITTED (datetime — "2026-03-28 06:01" — two tokens) - Then: TOT.DURATION, JOB.DURATION, #RECOVERIES, STATUS, POOL
CRITICAL: The REQUESTED column can contain spaces ("1x L4:1"). The SUBMITTED column is always two tokens (date + time). Parsing with simple split() requires finding the STATUS column by searching for known status values, which the existing parse_job_status() in ralph_monitor.py already does correctly.
For the monitor, we need to also parse: - TOT.DURATION: Total wall-clock time since submission (includes queuing) - JOB.DURATION: Actual running time (excludes queuing, "-" when PENDING) - #RECOVERIES: Number of spot preemption recoveries - NAME: To match against manifest entries
SkyPilot Python API alternative: `sky.jobs.queue()` returns a list of dicts. However, this requires importing sky, which may not be installed on all machines. The subprocess approach (parsing CLI output) is more portable.
IMPORTANT: The monitor should handle the case where `sky` CLI is not available or the SkyPilot API server is down. Graceful degradation: log the error, wait for next poll cycle, do not crash.
Cost data: SkyPilot does NOT report per-job cost. The monitor must compute it: duration_hours * hourly_rate (from GPU type + spot/on-demand). Hourly rates should come from the manifest, not hardcoded.

Prefect 3.x can absolutely be used for monitoring flows. The architecture:
1. Create a `monitor_flow` that wraps the monitor_factorial.py logic 2. Deploy it as a local Prefect deployment (no cloud agent needed) 3. Use Prefect's @task decorator for each polling cycle 4. Use Prefect's automations for alerting on flow state changes 5. The flow run appears in Prefect UI — researcher can check status
However, for Phase 1, Prefect integration adds unnecessary complexity. The standalone script should work first. Prefect wrapping comes in Phase 3.
Phase 3 integration points: - The monitor_flow imports and calls the same detection logic from monitor_factorial.py - Prefect tasks provide automatic retries, state persistence, and UI visibility - Prefect automations can trigger Slack/email when the monitor_flow itself fails - This is the "who watches the watchmen" layer
Prefect deployment: `prefect deployment run 'monitor-flow/factorial'` The researcher starts this alongside run_factorial.sh. If the Prefect agent crashes, Prefect Cloud (if configured) can alert. If self-hosted, the cron fallback (Phase 1) catches it.
IMPORTANT: The monitor flow MUST NOT be a long-running Prefect flow. Instead, use a Prefect deployment with a schedule (every 30s) that runs the check once and exits. This avoids Prefect's heartbeat timeout issues with long-running flows.

The researcher's workflow after this plan:
1. Launch: `./scripts/run_factorial.sh configs/factorial/debug.yaml` → Script auto-generates manifest at outputs/factorial_manifest.json → Script prints: "Monitor: make monitor-factorial"
2. Monitor: `make monitor-factorial` (in a tmux pane or separate terminal) → Prints live status table every 30s → Desktop notification on any anomaly → Auto-cancels stuck jobs → Writes structured log to outputs/job_monitor.jsonl → Exit code 0 = all succeeded, 1 = failures, 2 = budget exceeded
3. Walk away. Come back. Check terminal or read JSONL for what happened.
Notification methods (Linux): - notify-send: Desktop notification (best for when researcher is at the machine) - Terminal bell (\a): Works in all terminals, even over SSH - Console output: Always available, structured table format
Minimal config: The monitor reads EVERYTHING from the manifest JSON. No separate config file. The manifest contains job IDs, expected durations, hourly rates, budget cap, warn/cancel thresholds. One file = one experiment = one monitor.
The Makefile target should be dead simple: ```makefile monitor-factorial: uv run python scripts/monitor_factorial.py \ --manifest outputs/factorial_manifest.json \ --poll-interval 30 ```
CRITICAL DevEx rule: The monitor must NEVER require Claude Code to be running. If the researcher can't use it without Claude, it's not monitoring — it's another skill, and skills are the problem.

council-review.consensus:
All 5 experts agree on the phased approach with Phase 1 (cron-based standalone monitor) as the immediate priority. The monitor must:
1. Be a STANDALONE Python script — zero Claude Code dependency 2. Read all configuration from a JSON manifest 3. Parse sky jobs queue output using str.split() (no regex) 4. Detect 5 anomaly types: PENDING timeout, RUNNING timeout, cascading FAILED, FAILED_SETUP, budget exceeded 5. Send desktop notifications via notify-send + terminal bell 6. Write structured JSONL log for post-hoc analysis 7. Auto-cancel stuck jobs via sky jobs cancel 8. Be idempotent (PID file guard for cron safety) 9. Handle SkyPilot CLI unavailability gracefully
Average expert score: 4.32/5.00 Confidence: HIGH — all failure modes are well-documented and prevention rules are simple threshold checks.

phase.name: Cron-Based Factorial Monitor
phase.description:
Autonomous monitoring script that survives session loss and detects ALL observed failure modes within 60 seconds. This is the PRIMARY deliverable.
Architecture: run_factorial.sh launches jobs → writes manifest JSON monitor_factorial.py reads manifest → polls sky jobs queue → detects anomalies → sends notifications → writes JSONL log → auto-cancels stuck jobs
Detection rules (from metalearning): - PENDING > 30 min → WARN (capacity alarm) - RUNNING > warn_duration (3x expected) → WARN - RUNNING > cancel_duration (max_duration) → auto-CANCEL - 3+ identical FAILED in 5 min → KILL SWITCH - >50% of batch FAILED → KILL SWITCH - FAILED_SETUP → immediate HIGH alert - Total cost > budget warning threshold → WARN - Total cost > budget cap → CANCEL ALL
The manifest JSON is the single source of truth for the monitor. Expected durations, budget caps, hourly rates — everything in the manifest.

task.title: Write manifest + data model tests
task.description: Define the manifest JSON schema and data model via failing tests. The manifest is generated by create_job_manifest.py (T1.5) and consumed by monitor_factorial.py (T1.3). Tests validate parsing, serialization, and schema enforcement.

tests.test: JobRecord dataclass can be constructed from a dict with all required fields: job_id, condition_name, model, loss, fold, spot, expected_duration_minutes, warn_duration_minutes, cancel_duration_minutes, hourly_rate, status, launched_at.
JobRecord serializes to dict and deserializes back identically.
JobManifest loads from a JSON file with header (experiment_name, budget_warning, budget_cap, launched_at) and jobs list.
JobManifest writes to JSON and reads back identically.
JobManifest raises ValueError when a required field is missing (e.g., no budget_cap). Never silently defaults.
JobManifest.job_count returns correct count.
JobManifest.active_jobs returns only non-terminal jobs.
JobManifest.terminal_jobs returns only terminal jobs.
JobManifest.total_cost computes sum of elapsed_hours * hourly_rate for all jobs with known duration.
JobManifest.success_rate = SUCCEEDED / total terminal jobs.
JobRecord.elapsed_minutes computes minutes since launched_at. Returns 0 if launched_at is None.
JobRecord.cost_usd = elapsed_hours * hourly_rate. Correct for partial hours (e.g., 15 min at $0.80/h = $0.20).

task.title: Implement manifest data model
task.description:
Implement JobRecord and JobManifest dataclasses.
JobRecord fields: job_id: int | None (None before launch, filled by run_factorial.sh) condition_name: str (e.g., "dynunet-dice_ce-calibfalse-f0") model: str loss: str fold: int spot: bool expected_duration_minutes: float warn_duration_minutes: float (typically 3x expected) cancel_duration_minutes: float (hard max) hourly_rate: float (USD per hour for this GPU + spot/on-demand) status: str (PENDING, STARTING, RUNNING, SUCCEEDED, FAILED, etc.) launched_at: str | None (ISO 8601 UTC timestamp) started_at: str | None (when status first became RUNNING) ended_at: str | None (when status became terminal) failure_category: str | None (from ralph_monitor diagnosis) recovery_count: int (number of spot preemption recoveries)
JobManifest fields: experiment_name: str config_file: str (path to factorial YAML) budget_warning_usd: float budget_cap_usd: float pending_timeout_minutes: float (default 30) kill_switch_threshold: int (default 3 — identical failures before kill) kill_switch_window_minutes: float (default 5) batch_failure_pct: float (default 0.5 — >50% failed triggers kill) launched_at: str (ISO 8601 UTC) jobs: list[JobRecord]
Methods on JobManifest: from_json(path) -> JobManifest to_json(path) -> None (atomic write: write to .tmp then rename) job_count -> int active_jobs -> list[JobRecord] terminal_jobs -> list[JobRecord] total_cost -> float success_rate -> float all_terminal -> bool
Methods on JobRecord: from_dict(d) -> JobRecord to_dict() -> dict elapsed_minutes -> float (from launched_at to now or ended_at) cost_usd -> float (elapsed_hours * hourly_rate)
IMPORTANT: Use dataclasses, not dicts. Use pathlib.Path for files. Use datetime.now(timezone.utc). No regex.

task.title: Write SkyPilot queue parser tests
task.description:
Tests for parsing multi-job sky jobs queue output. The existing parse_job_status in ralph_monitor.py handles single-job lookup. We need batch parsing: extract ALL jobs with their full metadata (ID, NAME, TOT.DURATION, JOB.DURATION, #RECOVERIES, STATUS).
Use ACTUAL sky jobs queue output from 11th pass as test fixtures.

tests.test: Empty or header-only output returns empty list.
Single RUNNING job correctly parsed with all fields.
Multiple jobs with PENDING, RUNNING, SUCCEEDED, FAILED parsed correctly.
PENDING job has JOB.DURATION = None (displayed as "-" in output).
TOT.DURATION "1h 22m" parsed to 82 minutes. "45m" parsed to 45 minutes. "2h" parsed to 120 minutes. "5m" parsed to 5 minutes.
JOB.DURATION parsed correctly. "-" becomes None.
#RECOVERIES column parsed as integer.
NAME field extracted correctly (e.g., "dynunet-dice_ce-calibfalse-f0").
FAILED_SETUP is a distinct status from FAILED.
RECOVERING status is recognized (spot preemption recovery in progress).
When sky CLI returns error text (not job table), returns empty list and does not crash.
Full realistic output from 11th pass (3 PENDING jobs) parses correctly.

task.title: Implement SkyPilot queue batch parser
task.description:
Implement parse_jobs_queue(output: str) -> list[QueuedJob].
QueuedJob dataclass: job_id: int name: str requested: str (e.g., "1x L4:1") submitted_at: str total_duration_minutes: float | None job_duration_minutes: float | None recovery_count: int status: str
Duration parsing: "1h 22m" → 82.0, "45m" → 45.0, "2h" → 120.0, "5m" → 5.0, "-" → None. Use str.split() and conditional logic, NOT regex (Rule 16).
Strategy for parsing the tabular output: 1. Split into lines 2. Skip header lines (contain "Managed jobs", "ID", dashes) 3. For each data line, split by whitespace 4. Column 0 = ID (must be an integer) 5. Column 1 = TASK (skip) 6. Column 2 = NAME 7. Find STATUS by searching for known status values from the right 8. Parse duration columns relative to STATUS position 9. Handle the REQUESTED column's potential spaces ("1x L4:1")
IMPORTANT: This is an extension of the existing parse_job_status in ralph_monitor.py. Consider importing the known_statuses set from there to avoid duplication.

task.title: Write anomaly detection tests
task.description:
The anomaly detector is the CORE of the monitor. It takes a JobManifest (updated with current queue data) and returns a list of Alert objects. Each alert has a severity (INFO, WARN, CRITICAL, EMERGENCY) and an action (LOG, NOTIFY, CANCEL_JOB, CANCEL_ALL).
These tests validate EVERY failure mode from passes 4, 10, and 11.

tests.test: Job PENDING for 35 min (threshold 30 min) triggers WARN alert. Job PENDING for 25 min does NOT trigger.
Job RUNNING for 35 min with warn_duration 30 min triggers WARN. Job RUNNING for 25 min does NOT trigger.
Job RUNNING for 55 min with cancel_duration 50 min triggers CRITICAL with action=CANCEL_JOB.
Job with FAILED_SETUP status triggers HIGH alert immediately.
3 jobs FAILED with same failure_category within 5 min triggers EMERGENCY kill switch. 2 jobs does NOT trigger. 3 jobs spread over 10 min does NOT trigger (outside window).
12 of 20 jobs FAILED (60%) triggers EMERGENCY kill switch when threshold is 50%. 9 of 20 (45%) does NOT trigger.
Total cost $12 with budget_warning $10 triggers WARN. Total cost $8 does NOT trigger.
Total cost $16 with budget_cap $15 triggers EMERGENCY with action=CANCEL_ALL.
All jobs RUNNING within expected durations, cost within budget → empty alert list.
Two jobs stuck + budget warning → 3 alerts returned (not just the first).
Same alert (same job, same type) is NOT repeated on subsequent polls. Track already-fired alerts.
When all jobs are terminal, return INFO summary alert with success count, failure count, total cost.
Simulate Job #154: RUNNING for 720 min (12h) with expected 5 min. Both WARN (at 15 min) and CRITICAL/CANCEL (at max_duration) should fire.
Simulate 3 jobs PENDING for 600+ min. WARN should fire for each after 30 min. No CANCEL (PENDING jobs can't be meaningfully cancelled — but warn the user to consider region fallback).
Simulate 8 FAILED_SETUP with same error. Kill switch should trigger after the 3rd failure (within 5 min window).

task.title: Implement anomaly detector
task.description:
Implement AnomalyDetector class and Alert dataclass.
Alert dataclass: severity: str (INFO, WARN, HIGH, CRITICAL, EMERGENCY) alert_type: str (PENDING_TIMEOUT, DURATION_WARN, DURATION_CANCEL, FAILED_SETUP, KILL_SWITCH_IDENTICAL, KILL_SWITCH_BATCH, BUDGET_WARN, BUDGET_CAP, ALL_TERMINAL) action: str (LOG, NOTIFY, CANCEL_JOB, CANCEL_ALL, SUMMARY) job_id: int | None (None for batch-level alerts) message: str (human-readable description) timestamp: str (ISO 8601 UTC)
AnomalyDetector: __init__(manifest: JobManifest) check_all() -> list[Alert] _check_pending_timeouts() -> list[Alert] _check_duration_anomalies() -> list[Alert] _check_failed_setup() -> list[Alert] _check_kill_switch() -> list[Alert] _check_budget() -> list[Alert] _check_all_terminal() -> list[Alert] _already_fired: set[tuple] — tracks (job_id, alert_type) to deduplicate
Kill switch implementation: Track failure timestamps in a deque (sliding window). Group by failure_category. If count >= threshold within window → trigger. Also check batch failure percentage.
Thresholds come from the manifest (not hardcoded in the detector): manifest.pending_timeout_minutes job.warn_duration_minutes job.cancel_duration_minutes manifest.budget_warning_usd manifest.budget_cap_usd manifest.kill_switch_threshold manifest.kill_switch_window_minutes manifest.batch_failure_pct

task.title: Write alert dispatcher tests
task.description: The alert dispatcher receives Alert objects and routes them to appropriate notification channels: JSONL log (always), desktop notification (WARN+), terminal bell (CRITICAL+), console output (always).

tests.test: INFO alert written to JSONL with correct schema: timestamp, severity, alert_type, job_id, message, action.
Multiple alerts append to the same JSONL file (not overwrite).
WARN alert triggers subprocess call to notify-send with correct urgency and message. (Mock subprocess.run.)
INFO alert does NOT trigger notify-send.
If notify-send binary is not found, log a warning and continue (do NOT crash the monitor).
CRITICAL alert prints terminal bell character (\a) to stdout.
Alert with action=CANCEL_JOB triggers subprocess call to sky jobs cancel {job_id}. (Mock subprocess.run.)
Alert with action=CANCEL_ALL triggers sky jobs cancel for every active (non-terminal) job in the manifest.
If sky jobs cancel fails (e.g., job already terminal), log error and continue.
Status table printed to console has correct columns and formatting. Includes: job_id, name, status, elapsed, expected, cost.

task.title: Implement alert dispatcher
task.description:
Implement AlertDispatcher class.
AlertDispatcher: __init__(jsonl_path: Path, manifest: JobManifest, sky_bin: str = "sky", enable_desktop: bool = True) dispatch(alert: Alert) -> None dispatch_batch(alerts: list[Alert]) -> None print_status_table() -> None _write_jsonl(alert: Alert) -> None _send_desktop_notification(alert: Alert) -> None _send_terminal_bell() -> None _cancel_job(job_id: int) -> bool _cancel_all_active() -> None _print_console(alert: Alert) -> None
notify-send integration: subprocess.run(["notify-send", "--urgency={urgency}", title, body], check=False, capture_output=True) urgency mapping: WARN→normal, HIGH→normal, CRITICAL→critical, EMERGENCY→critical If FileNotFoundError (notify-send not installed), log once and disable.
sky jobs cancel integration: subprocess.run([sky_bin, "jobs", "cancel", str(job_id), "-y"], check=False, capture_output=True, text=True) Log success/failure. Continue on failure.
Status table: Print a formatted table to stdout showing all jobs, their current status, elapsed time, expected time, and cost. Use simple string formatting (no external table library dependency).

task.title: Write manifest generator tests
task.description: The manifest generator reads the factorial config YAML and the job log from run_factorial.sh to produce the manifest JSON that the monitor consumes.

tests.test: Given configs/factorial/debug.yaml (or a test fixture copy), generates a manifest with correct number of jobs (models * losses * calibs * folds).

DynUNet → 10 min expected, 30 min warn, 50 min cancel. SAM3 Hybrid → 25 min expected, 75 min warn, 75 min cancel. SAM3 Vanilla → 15 min expected, 45 min warn, 75 min cancel. SAM3 TopoLoRA → 25 min expected, 75 min warn, 75 min cancel. VesselFM → 15 min expected, 45 min warn, 75 min cancel. MambaVesselNet → 12 min expected, 36 min warn, 60 min cancel.
SAM3 Hybrid and SAM3 TopoLoRA have spot=false (from model_overrides). DynUNet has spot=true (default).
Spot rate and on-demand rate differ. Manifest uses correct rate based on spot flag.
Budget warning and cap are configurable. Defaults: warning=$8, cap=$15 for debug experiments.
Parse run_factorial.sh job log to extract job IDs for each condition. Format: "CONDITION_ID | model | loss | aux_calib | fold | LAUNCHED"
Before jobs are launched, job_id is None for all records. Monitor handles this gracefully (matches by condition_name instead).
Zero-shot baselines (sam3_vanilla, vesselfm) from the factorial config are included in the manifest with their expected durations.

task.title: Implement manifest generator
task.description:
Two components:
1. src/minivess/compute/manifest_generator.py — Pure library code. generate_manifest(config_path: Path, job_log_path: Path | None = None, gpu_rates: dict | None = None) -> JobManifest
Expected duration lookup table (from 11th pass calibration): MODEL_EXPECTED_DURATIONS = { "dynunet": {"expected": 10, "warn": 30, "cancel": 50}, "sam3_hybrid": {"expected": 25, "warn": 75, "cancel": 75}, "sam3_vanilla": {"expected": 15, "warn": 45, "cancel": 75}, "sam3_topolora": {"expected": 25, "warn": 75, "cancel": 75}, "vesselfm": {"expected": 15, "warn": 45, "cancel": 75}, "mambavesselnet": {"expected": 12, "warn": 36, "cancel": 60}, }
GPU hourly rates (GCP L4, from pricing): DEFAULT_GPU_RATES = { "L4_spot": 0.28, # GCP L4 spot price per hour "L4_ondemand": 0.80, # GCP L4 on-demand price per hour }
NOTE: These are DEFAULT values that can be overridden via the factorial YAML's infrastructure section (Rule 29 compliance — not hardcoded in function signatures, but module-level named constants that the config can override).
Reads factorial config YAML → extracts factors → generates JobRecord for each cell → wraps in JobManifest.
If job_log_path is provided, parse the run_factorial.sh job log to match condition names to SkyPilot job IDs.
2. scripts/create_job_manifest.py — CLI wrapper. Usage: uv run python scripts/create_job_manifest.py \ --config configs/factorial/debug.yaml \ --job-log outputs/20260328T090000Z_factorial_job_ids.txt \ --output outputs/factorial_manifest.json
Can also be called without --job-log (pre-launch manifest with job_id=None).

task.title: Write monitor main loop tests
task.description: Integration-level tests for the monitor's main loop. Mock the SkyPilot CLI calls but test the full detect→alert→update→sleep cycle.

tests.test: One poll cycle: reads manifest, polls queue, updates manifest, runs detector, dispatches alerts. All steps execute in order.
Monitor loop exits (returns exit code 0) when all jobs reach terminal status and all SUCCEEDED.
Monitor loop exits with code 1 when all jobs terminal but some FAILED.
Monitor loop exits with code 2 when budget cap is exceeded.
If sky jobs queue fails (subprocess error), log warning and retry on next cycle. Do NOT crash.
If PID file exists with a running process, monitor exits immediately with informative message.
PID file is removed on normal exit and on signal (SIGINT, SIGTERM).
After each poll, manifest JSON is updated atomically (write to .tmp, then rename). Concurrent readers never see a half-written file.
Console status table is printed after each poll cycle.
When manifest jobs have job_id=None, monitor matches queue entries by condition_name instead.

task.title: Implement monitor_factorial.py CLI
task.description:
The main monitor script. Standalone, no Claude Code dependency.
Usage: uv run python scripts/monitor_factorial.py \ --manifest outputs/factorial_manifest.json \ --poll-interval 30
Main loop (pseudocode): 1. Parse args (--manifest, --poll-interval, --dry-run) 2. Check PID file — exit if another instance running 3. Write PID file 4. Register signal handlers (SIGINT, SIGTERM → cleanup PID file) 5. Load manifest from JSON 6. Loop: a. Run sky jobs queue (subprocess) b. Parse queue output → list[QueuedJob] c. Update manifest with current status/duration d. Run AnomalyDetector.check_all() → list[Alert] e. Run AlertDispatcher.dispatch_batch(alerts) f. Write updated manifest to disk (atomic) g. Print status table h. If all terminal → print summary, exit with appropriate code i. Sleep poll_interval seconds 7. Clean up PID file
Exit codes: 0 = All jobs terminal and ALL SUCCEEDED 1 = All jobs terminal but some FAILED 2 = Budget exceeded (emergency stop) 130 = SIGINT (Ctrl+C)
The script imports from src/minivess/compute/ (job_manifest, sky_queue_parser, anomaly_detector, alert_dispatcher). All logic is in library code — the script is just the CLI + main loop.
PID file: outputs/.monitor_factorial.pid JSONL log: outputs/job_monitor.jsonl

task.title: Integrate manifest generation into run_factorial.sh
task.description:
After all jobs are launched and the "Trainable conditions" summary is printed, add:
1. Generate the manifest JSON by calling create_job_manifest.py with --config and --job-log (the job log file already exists). 2. Print the monitor command to stdout: "Monitor: make monitor-factorial" "Or: uv run python scripts/monitor_factorial.py --manifest outputs/factorial_manifest.json --poll-interval 30"
Do NOT auto-start the monitor. The researcher decides when and how to run it (foreground, tmux, cron, etc.).
Also add a --manifest-only flag that generates the manifest without launching jobs (for testing the monitor with mocked data).

task.title: Add Makefile monitor-factorial target
task.description:
Add to the Makefile:
```makefile monitor-factorial: ## Monitor running factorial experiment (30s poll) uv run python scripts/monitor_factorial.py \ --manifest outputs/factorial_manifest.json \ --poll-interval 30 ```
Place it near the existing `monitor-smoke-test` target for discoverability.

task.title: Add monitor pre-launch gate to experiment harness skill
task.description:
Add a new rule H10 to the experiment harness:
H10: MONITOR-BEFORE-LAUNCH Before ANY sky jobs launch, ensure: 1. scripts/monitor_factorial.py EXISTS and is executable 2. Print the monitor command to the user 3. In the experiment XML, add <monitoring> section with the exact command to start the monitor
This is informational (the harness can't verify the monitor is running), but it ensures the researcher is REMINDED every time.
Also update the Phase 2 VALIDATE gate table to include: | Monitor script | `scripts/monitor_factorial.py` exists | File exists |

phase.name: GCP Budget Alerts
phase.description:
Cloud-native cost safety net using GCP Budget Alerts. Operates at the GCP project billing level — works even if the researcher's machine is off, the monitor script crashes, and all Claude sessions are dead.
Limitation: ~15 minute latency. This is a CEILING, not a per-job monitor. It catches runaway cost even if Phase 1 fails entirely.

task.title: Write Pulumi budget alert tests
task.description: Test that the Pulumi GCP stack includes budget alert resources. These are structural tests — they verify the Pulumi program creates the expected resources, not that GCP actually accepts them.

tests.test: The Pulumi GCP program defines a gcp.billing.Budget resource (or equivalent) with the project's billing account.
Budget alert has thresholds at 50%, 90%, 100% of the defined budget amount.
Budget alert sends notifications (email or Pub/Sub).
Budget amounts for debug ($15), production ($50), hard cap ($100) are configurable via Pulumi config, not hardcoded.

task.title: Implement Pulumi budget alert resource
task.description:
Add GCP Budget Alert resources to the Pulumi GCP stack.
Three budget levels (configurable via Pulumi config): - debug_budget: $15 (default for debug factorial experiments) - production_budget: $50 (for full factorial experiments) - hard_cap_budget: $100 (absolute maximum, billing alarm)
Each budget has threshold rules at 50%, 90%, 100%. Notification via email to the researcher's email address (configurable via Pulumi config: researcher_email).
Uses gcp.billing.Budget resource (Pulumi GCP provider).
IMPORTANT: This requires the billing account ID, which should already be configured in the Pulumi stack. Check existing __main__.py for how billing is referenced.

task.title: Document budget alerts in deployment CLAUDE.md
task.description: Add a "Budget Alerts" section to the GCP deployment documentation: - What thresholds are configured - How to change budget amounts (Pulumi config) - Expected notification latency (~15 minutes) - How this complements the local monitor (Phase 1)
task.verify: Read deployment/pulumi/gcp/CLAUDE.md to confirm section exists

phase.name: Prefect Monitor Flow
phase.description:
Wrap the Phase 1 monitor logic in a Prefect flow for UI visibility, automation triggers, and state persistence. The Prefect monitor flow is the "who watches the watchmen" layer — if the cron/tmux monitor crashes, Prefect automations can detect it.
Architecture: Prefect deployment "monitor-flow/factorial" runs locally (no cloud GPU). Each Prefect task is one poll cycle. Prefect tracks task success/failure. If the flow itself fails, Prefect automations fire a notification. Researcher can check Prefect UI for experiment monitoring status.
IMPORTANT: This wraps Phase 1 code — it does NOT duplicate it. The detection logic lives in src/minivess/compute/ and is imported by both scripts/monitor_factorial.py and the Prefect flow.

task.title: Write Prefect monitor flow tests
task.description: Tests for the Prefect monitor flow. These test the flow structure and task composition, not the detection logic (already tested in Phase 1).

tests.test: monitor_flow is decorated with @flow and can be called.
poll_and_check is decorated with @task.
Flow reads manifest from the configured path.
Flow returns a MonitorSummary when all jobs are terminal.
Flow raises RuntimeError when budget cap is exceeded, causing Prefect to mark the flow run as FAILED.
If sky CLI fails, the poll task retries (Prefect retry config).

task.title: Implement Prefect monitor flow
Add Prefect automation documentation
task.description:
Document how to set up Prefect automations that fire when the monitor flow itself fails or crashes:
1. Create a Prefect automation trigger on monitor-flow FAILED state 2. Action: Send email/webhook notification 3. This catches the case where the monitor itself dies
Also document the Prefect deployment creation command.

phase.name: Claude Code Scheduled Triggers
phase.description:
The "luxury" layer: Claude Code sessions scheduled via /schedule to periodically read the monitor's output files, perform root cause analysis, and update the experiment report.
This is Phase 4 because it requires Phases 1-3 to be stable first. Claude is the DIAGNOSTIC tool, not the MONITORING tool. The cron monitor (Phase 1) detects; Claude diagnoses.
Architecture: /schedule creates a cron entry → Claude headless session starts → reads outputs/job_monitor.jsonl → reads outputs/factorial_manifest.json → performs root cause analysis on any alerts → updates experiment report → session ends.

task.title: Document /schedule integration for monitoring
task.description:
Document how to use Claude Code's /schedule skill to create recurring monitoring sessions:
1. `/schedule` can invoke Claude every N minutes 2. Each invocation reads the manifest and JSONL log 3. Claude performs root cause analysis on any alerts 4. Writes findings to the experiment report 5. Creates GitHub issues for systemic problems
Include example /schedule invocation: /schedule every 30m "Read outputs/job_monitor.jsonl and outputs/factorial_manifest.json. Diagnose any WARN/CRITICAL alerts. Update the experiment report at docs/planning/..."
Limitations: - Each Claude session costs API credits - Minimum practical interval: ~15-30 minutes - Session may fail (token limit, network) - NOT a replacement for Phase 1 cron monitor

task.title: Create scheduled task template
task.description:
Template YAML for a scheduled monitoring trigger. This is NOT executed by any automation — it's a template that the researcher copies and adapts when setting up /schedule for a specific experiment.
```yaml # Scheduled monitoring trigger template # Copy this and customize for your experiment. # Start with: /schedule every 30m "..." name: monitor-experiment schedule: every 30m prompt: | Read the following files: 1. outputs/job_monitor.jsonl (latest 20 lines) 2. outputs/factorial_manifest.json
For any alerts with severity WARN or higher: - Identify the root cause - Check if the alert pattern matches known metalearning docs - Recommend action (wait, cancel, fix, escalate)
Update the experiment report at: docs/planning/v0-2_archive/original_docs/run-debug-factorial-experiment-report-{N}th-pass.md
If budget exceeded: cancel all active jobs via sky jobs cancel. context: - .claude/metalearning/2026-03-27-no-job-duration-monitoring-12h-debug-run.md - .claude/metalearning/2026-03-28-10hr-pending-no-monitoring-intervention.md ```

task.title: Define structured alert file format for Claude consumption
task.description:
Extend the AlertDispatcher (from Phase 1 Task 1.4g) to also write a human-readable summary file at outputs/monitor_summary.md when alerts of severity WARN or higher are detected.
This file is what the scheduled Claude session reads. Format:
```markdown # Monitor Summary (auto-generated) Updated: 2026-03-28T10:30:00Z
## Active Alerts - [CRITICAL] Job 154 (sam3_hybrid-cbdice_cldice-calibtrue-f0) RUNNING 720 min, expected 25 min. Auto-cancelled. - [WARN] Budget at $12.50 / $15.00 cap (83%)
## Job Status | Job | Name | Status | Elapsed | Expected | Cost | | ... |
## Action Needed - Investigate root cause of Job 154 duration anomaly - Consider region fallback if PENDING jobs persist ```
This is a WRITE-ONLY file from the monitor's perspective. Claude reads it; the monitor writes it. No bidirectional communication.
task.verify: uv run pytest tests/v2/unit/monitoring/ -v --tb=short -k "summary or markdown"

testing-strategy.conftest-note: Create tests/v2/unit/monitoring/__init__.py and tests/v2/unit/monitoring/conftest.py with shared fixtures: - sample_manifest: A JobManifest with 5 realistic jobs (mixed status) - sample_queue_output: Realistic sky jobs queue text output - sample_11th_pass_queue: Exact queue output from 11th pass - tmp_manifest_path: Path to temporary manifest JSON (uses tmp_path) - mock_sky_command: Fixture that mocks subprocess.run for sky CLI calls

test-files.file: Data model: JobRecord, JobManifest serialization, computed properties
Queue output parsing: duration, status, name, edge cases
Anomaly detection: all 5 root causes, scenario replay (passes 4/10/11)
Alert routing: JSONL, notify-send, terminal bell, sky cancel
Manifest generation: config parsing, duration lookup, job ID matching
Main loop: poll cycle, exit codes, PID guard, graceful degradation
Pulumi budget alert: resource exists, thresholds, notifications
Prefect flow: structure, retry, summary, budget failure

expected-test-count.phase: 0 tests (documentation only, plus 1-2 extending Phase 1 tests)

testing-strategy.acceptance-gate: Run ALL new tests: uv run pytest tests/v2/unit/monitoring/ -v --tb=short Run staging gate: make test-staging (must still pass with new tests added) Replay scenarios: Tests for passes 4, 10, 11 must ALL detect anomalies within one simulated poll cycle.

integration-points.integration: After all jobs are launched, run_factorial.sh calls create_job_manifest.py to generate the manifest JSON. The job log file (already written by run_factorial.sh) provides condition-to-job-ID mapping.

Monitor calls `sky jobs queue` via subprocess. Parses tabular output using str.split() (no regex). Also calls `sky jobs cancel {id} -y` for auto-cancellation. Must handle CLI unavailability gracefully.
Desktop notifications via Linux notify-send. Must handle the binary not being installed (e.g., headless server). Degrades to terminal-only.

The harness skill's Phase 2 VALIDATE gate checks that scripts/monitor_factorial.py exists. The harness's Phase 3 EXECUTE prints the monitor command. No runtime coupling — the monitor is independent infrastructure.

The monitor writes outputs/job_monitor.jsonl and outputs/factorial_manifest.json. Claude Code sessions (manual or scheduled) read these files for diagnosis and report updates. One-way data flow: monitor writes, Claude reads.

integration.name: Prefect monitor flow → Phase 1 library code

integration-points.integration: The Prefect flow imports from src/minivess/compute/ (same modules as the CLI script). Zero code duplication. The flow adds Prefect's task tracking, retries, and UI visibility on top.
GCP-native alerting. Independent of all local infrastructure. 15-minute latency. Catches runaway cost if everything else fails.

step.description: Run full Phase 1 tests: uv run pytest tests/v2/unit/monitoring/ -v AND make test-staging
Full test suite: make test-staging AND make test-prod

rollback-plan.description:
Each phase is independently deployable. If Phase 1 is problematic: - The monitor is a standalone script — delete it and nothing breaks - run_factorial.sh changes are additive (manifest generation after launch) - The Makefile target is additive - The experiment harness gate is informational only
If Phase 2 (Pulumi) is problematic: - `pulumi destroy` the budget alert resources - No impact on the rest of the monitoring stack
If Phase 3 (Prefect) is problematic: - Don't deploy the monitor flow — Phase 1 cron still works - Prefect integration is an overlay, not a replacement
If Phase 4 (Claude schedule) is problematic: - Delete the scheduled trigger — Phase 1 cron still works - Claude diagnosis is a luxury layer

rollback-commands.command: cd deployment/pulumi/gcp && pulumi destroy --target budget-alert-debug --target budget-alert-production --target budget-alert-hard-cap

criterion.description: Monitor detects 10th pass scenario (12h stuck job) within 1 poll cycle
Monitor detects 11th pass scenario (10h PENDING) within 1 poll cycle
Monitor detects 4th pass scenario (8 FAILED_SETUP) within 1 poll cycle
Monitor sends desktop notification on WARN and above
Monitor works without Claude Code running

success-criteria.criterion: Verified by: script is standalone Python with no Claude Code imports

criterion.description: Monitor handles SkyPilot CLI failure gracefully
Staging gate passes with new tests
Pulumi stack includes budget alert resources
Prefect monitor flow callable and testable
